11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
15	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
16	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
17	  \uses{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
18	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
19	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
20	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
21	  % L933--938).
22	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
23	  % equality
24	  % $$
25	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
26	  % $$
27	  % as $R'$-modules."
28	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
29	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
30	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
31	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
32	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
33	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
34	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
35	  \[
36	    \operatorname{regroup} :
37	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
38	  \]
39	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
40	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
41	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
42	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
43	  hypothesis.
44	\end{lemma}
45	
46	\begin{proof}
47	  \uses{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
48	  This is pure tensor algebra, assembled from Mathlib's heterobasic equivalences
49	  with no geometry:
50	  \[
51	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M
52	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \texttt{comm}\ }\; M \otimes_A (A \otimes_R R')
53	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\ }\; M \otimes_R R'
54	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \texttt{comm}\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
55	  \]
56	  The first and last arrows are the \(R'\)-linear heterobasic commutativity
57	  isomorphisms \(\texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.comm}\) (the \(R'\)-action
58	  enters through the \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor, so the \(R'\)-linear versions apply);
59	  the middle arrow is \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) of
60	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}, instantiated at the tower
61	  \(R \to A \to A\) with module \(M\) and base-change object \(R'\). On the generator
62	  \[
63	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\mapsto\; m \otimes (a \otimes r')
64	    \;\xmapsto{\ \operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\ }\; (a \cdot m) \otimes r'
65	    \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
66	  \]
67	  using \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}(m \otimes_A (a \otimes_R r')) = (a \cdot m)
68	  \otimes_R r'\) (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\)). The \(R'\)-linearity of the
69	  composite is checked on generators by \(\texttt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\):
70	  the \(\texttt{rightAlgebra}\) action \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)
71	  combines with \(\texttt{comm\_tmul}\) and \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\) to
72	  transport the scalar through each factor. Being a composite of linear equivalences
73	  it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of the three factors carries a
74	  flatness hypothesis.
75	\end{proof}
76

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	\leanok
47	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
48	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
49	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
50	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
51	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
52	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
53	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
54	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
55	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
56	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
57	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
58	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
59	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
60	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
61	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
62	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
63	
64	  The formal target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic} regards \(M\) as an
65	  \(A\)-module through the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\), and packages the
66	  localisation \(M_f\) as the localised module at the powers of \(f\): there exists
67	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M}
68	  is free over \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
69	\end{theorem}
70	
71	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold pass creates the decl pinned above with
72	% exactly this signature):
73	%   (A B M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
74	%     [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]
75	%     [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
76	%     [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
77	%     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
78	%       Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M)
79	
80	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
81	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
82	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
83	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
84	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
85	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
86	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
87	% there exists a finite filtration
88	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
89	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
90	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
91	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
92	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
93	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
94	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
95	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
96	\begin{proof}
97	
98	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case as a thin Mathlib wrapper.}
99	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself (i.e.\ \(M\) is a finite
100	  \(A\)-module, not merely a finite \(B\)-module). Since \(A\) is noetherian, a finite
101	  \(A\)-module is finitely presented over \(A\). Let \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) be the
102	  localisation of \(A\) at the multiplicative set \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\). The
103	  generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M = S^{-1} M\) is a \emph{finite-dimensional}
104	  \(K\)-vector space, hence free over \(K\); thus the freeness hypothesis at the
105	  generic localisation is automatic, with no input from the structure of \(B\). The
106	  result is then exactly Mathlib's
107	  \texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers}
108	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Free}): a finitely-presented \(A\)-module
109	  that is free over the localisation \(A_S = K\) becomes free over
110	  \texttt{Localization.Away}~\(r\) after inverting some \(r \in S\), i.e.\ some
111	  \(r \in A\), \(r \ne 0\). Setting \(f := r\) gives \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). (The
112	  openness of the free locus is the \texttt{Module.freeLocus} API of
113	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Spectrum.Prime.FreeLocus}, which records that the locus
114	  where \(M\) is free over \(A\) is open and, by the above, non-empty.) This closes
115	  the theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
116	
117	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
118	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
119	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
120	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
121	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
122	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
123	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
124	  for exactly this residue.
125	
126	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction), as a \texttt{\textbackslash uses}-linked chain.}
127	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
128	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
129	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
130	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
131	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
132	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
133	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
134	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
135	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
136	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
137	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
138	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
139	\end{proof}
140	
141	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
142	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
143	
144	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
145	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
146	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
147	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each
148	\texttt{\textbackslash uses} only earlier blocks (or Mathlib anchors). Throughout,
149	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
150	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
151	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
152	
153	\begin{lemma}\leanok
154	[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
155	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
156	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
157	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
158	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
159	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
160	\end{lemma}
161	
162	\begin{proof}
163	
164	\leanok
165	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction and is already closed in Lean
166	  axiom-clean. A finite module over a noetherian ring is finitely presented, so
167	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
168	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
169	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
170	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
171	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
172	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
173	  \(f := r\).
174	\end{proof}
175	
176	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
177	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
178	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
179	  \mathlibok
180	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
181	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
182	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
183	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
184	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r = \texttt{Localization.Away}~r\) (of the same finite
185	  rank).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	\begin{lemma}\leanok
189	[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
190	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
191	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
192	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
193	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
194	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
195	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
196	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
197	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
198	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
199	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
200	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
201	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
202	  induction).}
203	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
204	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
205	  \(A_f\).
206	\end{lemma}
207	
208	\begin{proof}
209	
210	\leanok
211	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
212	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
213	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
214	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
215	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
216	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
217	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
218	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
219	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
220	\end{proof}
221	
222	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
223	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
224	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
225	  \mathlibok
226	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
227	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
228	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
229	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
230	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
231	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
232	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
233	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
234	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
235	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
236	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
237	\end{lemma}
238	
239	\begin{lemma}\leanok
240	[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
241	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
242	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
243	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
244	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
245	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
246	  exact sequence
247	  \[
248	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
249	  \]
250	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular the localised map \(i_f\) is injective and the
251	  localised map \(q_f\) is surjective. Here \(A_f = \texttt{Localization.Away}~f\)
252	  and \(M_f = \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers}~f)\,M\), the localisation
253	  taken at the submonoid of powers of \(f\) after restricting scalars to \(A\).
254	\end{lemma}
255	
256	\begin{proof}
257	
258	\leanok
259	  Localisation of modules at a multiplicative set is an exact functor: it preserves
260	  injections, surjections, and exactness at the middle term. Applying it to the
261	  scalar-restricted maps \(i\) and \(q\) (regarded as \(A\)-linear via the tower
262	  \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence, exact in all three positions. In the
263	  formalisation this is the localisation-preserves-exactness API
264	  (\texttt{LocalizedModule.map\_exact}) applied to \(i\) and \(q\) regarded as
265	  \(A\)-linear maps via the scalar restriction along \(A \to B\).
266	\end{proof}
267	
268	\begin{lemma}\leanok
269	[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
270	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
271	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
272	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
273	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
274	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
275	\end{lemma}
276	
277	\begin{proof}
278	
279	\leanok
280	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
281	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
282	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
283	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
284	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
285	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
286	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
287	\end{proof}
288	
289	\begin{lemma}\leanok
290	[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
291	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
292	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
293	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
294	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
295	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
296	\end{lemma}
297	
298	\begin{proof}
299	
300	\leanok
301	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
302	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
303	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
304	\end{proof}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}\leanok
307	[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence (assembly)]
308	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
310	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
311	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
312	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark
313	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1737-L1745).
314	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
315	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
316	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
317	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
318	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
319	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form
320	  %  $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. This reduces us to the case where $B$
321	  %  is a domain and $M=B$."
322	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (splicing fact).}
323	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
324	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
325	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation
326	  \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
327	\end{lemma}
328	
329	\begin{proof}
330	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
331	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
332	        \leanok
333	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
334	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
335	  Set \(f := f' f''\); since \(A\) is a domain and \(f', f'' \ne 0\), the product is
336	  non-zero, so \(f \ne 0\). Localising the given short exact sequence at the powers
337	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
338	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
339	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
340	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the
341	  factorisation \(f = f' f''\) (using that \(M'_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\)) shows
342	  \(M'_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and applying the same lemma to \(M''\) with the
343	  factorisation \(f = f'' f'\) (using that \(M''_{f''}\) is free over \(A_{f''}\))
344	  shows \(M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Finally
345	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split} concludes that the middle term \(M_f\) is
346	  free over \(A_f\).
347	\end{proof}
348	
349	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
350	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
351	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
352	  \mathlibok
353	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
354	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
355	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
356	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
357	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
358	\end{lemma}
359	
360	\begin{lemma}\leanok
361	[L4 — Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators]
362	  \label{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}
363	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial}
364	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
365	  % NOTE (resolved iter-006): the LEAN SIGNATURE block below now matches the landed
366	  % Lean decl `exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial`, which RETAINS the binder
367	  % `(_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))` as
368	  % the 3rd existential (after `(_ : g ≠ 0)`, before `φ`) — required to type the
369	  % AlgHom target as an A_g-algebra. The MATH statement is unaffected.
370	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (landed target, AlgHom encoding). Write
371	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g,   B_g := Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g).
372	  % The conclusion is:
373	  %   ∃ (n : ℕ) (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
374	  %     (_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))
375	  %     (φ : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g)
376	  %           →ₐ[Localization.Away g] Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)),
377	  %     Function.Injective φ ∧
378	  %     (letI := φ.toAlgebra;
379	  %      Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g))
380	  %                    (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))
381	  % The polynomial subalgebra A_g[b_1,…,b_n] is realised as the DOMAIN
382	  % `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`, embedded into B_g by the algebra map φ; the
383	  % generators b_j are φ(X_j). Compared with an earlier draft using three anonymous
384	  % instance existentials (∃ (_ : Algebra A_g B_g) (_ : Algebra (MvPoly … A_g) B_g)
385	  % (_ : IsScalarTower …)), this form keeps only the single `Algebra A_g B_g` binder
386	  % needed to type B_g as an A_g-algebra: the single φ then carries the algebra map,
387	  % `Function.Injective φ` encodes algebraic independence of the b_j, and
388	  % `Module.Finite … over φ.toAlgebra` encodes module-finiteness of B_g over the
389	  % image polynomial subalgebra. Downstream, L5 (\cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core})
390	  % consumes a finite module over `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`; the AlgHom hands it
391	  % exactly that algebra structure via `φ.toAlgebra`, with no instance revival.
392	  % Why B_g and not B: generic flatness only needs the property after one
393	  % inversion g; the integral-dependence equations only clear denominators after
394	  % inverting g (see the SOURCE QUOTE PROOF below).
395	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
396	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
397	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
398	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
399	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
400	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
401	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
402	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
403	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
404	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
405	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
406	  normalisation with denominators).}
407	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
408	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
409	  \(A_g = \texttt{Localization.Away}~g\) and
410	  \(B_g = \texttt{Localization.Away (algebraMap } A\,B\,g)\) for the localisations
411	  away from \(g\) and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element
412	  \(g \in A\) with \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
413	  \[
414	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
415	  \]
416	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] = \texttt{MvPolynomial (Fin } n)~A_g\) is the
417	  polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is
418	  module-finite over the image polynomial subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity
419	  of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic independence of the generators
420	  \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and the module-finiteness is taken over the algebra
421	  structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\). Equivalently, the classical statement
422	  ``\(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)'' is encoded by the single map
423	  \(\varphi\): it both names the polynomial subalgebra (as the domain
424	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)) and carries the algebra structure under which \(B_g\) is
425	  finite.
426	\end{lemma}
427	
428	\begin{proof}
429	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
430	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
431	  Apply Noether normalisation over the field \(K\)
432	  (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
433	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
434	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
435	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
436	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)). Fix a finite
437	  set of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) and
438	  finitely many equations of integral dependence they satisfy; let \(g \in A\),
439	  \(g \ne 0\), be a common denominator of the (finitely many) coefficients of those
440	  equations. After inverting \(g\) all these integrality relations hold over
441	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the chosen generators generate \(B_g\) as a module
442	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
443	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement is
444	  the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\);
445	  the algebraic independence of the \(b_j\) over \(A_g\) (inherited from their
446	  independence over \(K\)) makes \(\varphi\) injective, and the module-finiteness just
447	  established is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over the image of \(\varphi\).
448	\end{proof}
449	
450	\begin{lemma}\leanok
451	[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
452	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
453	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
454	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base}
455	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
456	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
457	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
458	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
459	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
460	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
461	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
462	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
463	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
464	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
465	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
466	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
467	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
468	  core of the induction).}
469	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
470	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
471	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
472	
473	  This is the genuine Mathlib-absent residue of generic freeness: the bottom of the
474	  Nitsure \S4 induction, on which the whole finite-type case rests.
475	\end{lemma}
476	
477	\begin{proof}
478	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
479	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
480	  We induct on \(d\). For \(d = 0\) the ring is \(A\) itself and \(N\) is a finite
481	  \(A\)-module, so the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
482	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}. For \(d \ge 1\), pass to \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) and
483	  set \(N_K = K \otimes_A N\). If \(N_K = 0\) the torsion base case
484	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} already produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\) free.
485	  Otherwise let \(m\) be the generic rank of \(N\) over the domain
486	  \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); choosing \(m\) elements of \(N\) whose images form a
487	  \(K(X_1, \dots, X_d)\)-basis of \(N_K\) and clearing denominators gives, after
488	  inverting some \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\), a short exact sequence of
489	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d]\)-modules
490	  \[
491	    0 \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d]^{\oplus m} \to N_g \to T \to 0
492	  \]
493	  with \(T\) a finite torsion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d]\)-module. Because \(T\) is
494	  torsion, its support over \(K[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) has dimension \(\le d - 1\), so
495	  \(T\) is finite over a polynomial ring in fewer variables after a further
496	  inversion; the inductive hypothesis yields \(h \ne 0\) with \(T_h\) free over
497	  \(A_{gh}\). The free middle term \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d]^{\oplus m}\) localises to a
498	  free \(A_{gh}\)-module by the \(d = 0\) leaf applied coordinatewise, and
499	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the two ends of the localised sequence to
500	  give \(N_f\) free over \(A_f\) with \(f := gh\).
501	\end{proof}
502	
503	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
504	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
505	
506	The following two file-internal helpers in the \texttt{GenericFreeness} namespace package
507	the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms (flatness and module-finiteness) that the
508	chain and the geometric globalization consume. Both are already proved axiom-clean.
509	
510	\begin{lemma}
511	
512	\leanok
513	[Flat localization away, finite module]
514	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
516	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
517	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
518	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} is
519	  \emph{flat} over \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
520	\end{lemma}
521	\begin{proof}
522	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
523	  \leanok
524	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} the localized module is free over
525	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}; free modules are flat (\texttt{Module.Flat.of\_free}).
526	\end{proof}
527	
528	\begin{lemma}
529	
530	\leanok
531	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
532	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
533	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
534	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
535	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a module-finite \(A\)-algebra
536	  (\texttt{[Module.Finite A B]}), and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module
537	  (\texttt{[Module.Finite B M]}) with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the
538	  scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such
539	  that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} is free over
540	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
541	\end{lemma}
542	\begin{proof}
543	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
544	  \leanok
545	  The \(A\)-finiteness of \(M\) is \emph{derived}, not assumed: since \(B\) is
546	  module-finite over \(A\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B\), composition along
547	  the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\) makes \(M\) module-finite over \(A\) (transitivity
548	  of module-finiteness, \texttt{Module.Finite.trans}). With \(M\) now a finite
549	  \(A\)-module, \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} produces the required \(f \ne 0\) with
550	  \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} free over
551	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}. The lemma is therefore strictly more general than a
552	  repackaging of \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}: the finite \(A\)-algebra \(B\) and the
553	  finite \(B\)-module \(M\) are the genuine inputs, and \(A\)-finiteness is a
554	  consequence of the hypotheses rather than one of them.
555	\end{proof}
556	
557	
558	\begin{theorem}
559	
560	\leanok
561	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
562	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
563	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
564	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
565	  % NOTE (re-signed iter-002): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
566	  % previously took `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, under which the
567	  % statement is FALSE (a non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any
568	  % non-empty open). It was re-signed in iter-002 (the decl is NOT in
569	  % archon-protected.yaml) to carry the coherence encoding below; the Lean file is
570	  % now the source of truth for the exact binders and the full flatness conclusion.
571	  %
572	  % Mathlib has no single `IsCoherent` predicate at this pin; coherence over a
573	  % locally noetherian base is encoded as the conjunction of two existing
574	  % SheafOfModules predicates on `F`:
575	  %     [F.IsQuasicoherent]   (SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent,
576	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent)
577	  %     [F.IsFiniteType]      (SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType,
578	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Generators)
579	  %
580	  % LEAN SIGNATURE HEADER (now landed in the .lean file):
581	  %   theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
582	  %       (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] (F : X.Modules)
583	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
584	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
585	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
586	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
587	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
588	
589	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
590	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
591	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
592	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
593	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
594	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
595	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
596	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
597	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
598	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
599	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
600	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
601	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
602	
603	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: without it the conclusion is
604	  false, since a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module need not become flat over any
605	  non-empty open of \(S\). The intended formal target therefore carries \(\F\)
606	  coherent over the locally noetherian base \(S\), which — absent a single
607	  \texttt{IsCoherent} predicate in Mathlib at this pin — is encoded as the
608	  conjunction of the two module-sheaf hypotheses \(\F\) quasi-coherent
609	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) and \(\F\) of finite type
610	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}), together with \(p\) locally of finite type
611	  and \(S\) integral and locally noetherian.
612	\end{theorem}
613	
614	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
615	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
616	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
617	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
618	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
619	% by restricting to a non-empty
620	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
621	\begin{proof}
622	  
623	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
624	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian; existence of such a \(U_0\)
625	  by \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset} applied at a point of the non-empty
626	  \(S\)). We assemble the witness \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
627	
628	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is locally of finite
629	  type it is in particular quasi-compact, so the open subscheme \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\)
630	  is quasi-compact; choose a finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of
631	  \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each
632	  ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
633	
634	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
635	  of \(\F\) (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the
636	  associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and
637	  finite type of \(\F\) (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}) makes \(M_j\) a finite
638	  \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\), \(M_j\) is the data fed
639	  to the algebraic form.
640	
641	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
642	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
643	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
644	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
645	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty (basic) open; over \(V\) every
646	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\) (freeness is preserved by the further localization
647	  \(A_{f_j} \to A_f\)).
648	
649	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness \(\Rightarrow\) flatness, checked locally.} On each patch the
650	  localized section module is free, hence flat over \(A_f\) by
651	  \texttt{Module.Flat.of\_free}; the fibrewise flatness conclusion (flatness of
652	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) over \(\Gamma(S, U)\) for affine \(U \le V\), \(W \le p^{-1}U\)) then
653	  follows from these local freeness statements by the localize-at-maximal-ideals
654	  flatness criterion \texttt{Module.flat\_of\_isLocalized\_maximal}. This produces the
655	  required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
656	\end{proof}
657	
658	\section{Mathlib status}
659	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
660	
661	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
662	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
663	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
664	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
665	generic-flatness argument consumes
666	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
667	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
668	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
669	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
670	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
671	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
672	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
673	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
674	are introduced in
675	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}; the
676	file-internal \texttt{GenericFreeness} namespace already lands the
677	finite-module / finite-morphism special case axiom-clean.
678

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	% NOTE: no `% archon:covers` line — these GR-cells/GR-glue declarations are destined
4	% for their own Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GrassmannianCells.lean (1:1 slug
5	% mapping), a deliberate split from QuotScheme.lean for parallel proving once QUOT-repr
6	% activates. QuotScheme.lean is covered solely by Picard_QuotScheme.tex.
7	
8	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter blueprints the FIRST TWO sub-phases (GR-cells,
9	% GR-glue) of the Grassmannian-scheme construction that ultimately underlies
10	% thm:grassmannian_representable in chapter Picard_QuotScheme. Following
11	% [Nitsure], \S 1 ("Construction of Grassmannian"), the scheme
12	% \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) is built directly by gluing
13	% \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)
14	% along explicit matrix-algebra transition maps, and shown smooth, separated
15	% and proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\). The later sub-phases (GR-quot: tautological
16	% quotient; GR-repr: functor-of-points; and the relative-over-\(S\) version
17	% with a vector bundle \(V\)) are OUT OF SCOPE here and are blueprinted with
18	% def:grassmannian_scheme / thm:grassmannian_representable in
19	% chapter Picard_QuotScheme. The declarations below are deliberately disjoint
20	% from that chapter's: this chapter adds only the construction lemmas for the
21	% absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\).
22	
23	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
24	\label{sec:gr_setup}
25	
26	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
27	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
28	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
29	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
30	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
31	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
32	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
33	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
34	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
35	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
36	
37	\begin{definition}
38	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
39	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
40	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
41	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
42	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
43	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
44	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
45	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
46	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
47	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
48	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
49	  %  $I$.
50	  %
51	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
52	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
53	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
54	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
55	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
56	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
57	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
58	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
59	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
60	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
61	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
62	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
63	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
64	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
65	  set
66	  \[
67	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
68	  \]
69	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
70	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
71	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
72	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
73	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
74	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
75	\end{definition}
76	
77	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
78	\label{sec:gr_transition}
79	
80	\begin{definition}
81	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
82	  \label{def:gr_transition}
83	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
84	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
85	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
86	  % affine patches" (read from
87	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
88	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
89	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
90	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
91	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
92	  %  Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
93	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
94	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
95	  %  on $U^I_J$.
96	  %
97	  %  For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
98	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
99	  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
100	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
101	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$.
102	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
103	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
104	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
105	  For \(I, J\) of cardinality \(d\), set
106	  \(P^I_J := \det(X^I_J) \in \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the determinant of the \(J\)-th
107	  minor of \(X^I\), and let
108	  \[
109	    U^I_J \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]
110	  \]
111	  be the principal open subscheme of \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) on
112	  which \(P^I_J\) is invertible; over \(U^I_J\) the matrix \(X^I_J\) is
113	  invertible, with inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) whose entries lie in
114	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) by Cramer's rule. Note \(P^I_I = 1\), so
115	  \(U^I_I = U^I\). The \emph{transition map} \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the ring
116	  homomorphism
117	  \[
118	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
119	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
120	    \qquad
121	    \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I,
122	  \]
123	  i.e.\ the indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) maps to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the
124	  \(d \times r\) matrix \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). This is well defined: the
125	  \(J\)-minor of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}\),
126	  matching the identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\), and the \(I\)-minor of
127	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), so
128	  \[
129	    \theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J
130	  \]
131	  is invertible in the target; hence \(\theta_{I,J}\) extends from
132	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]\) to the localisation \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\). Dually,
133	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
134	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\), which we also denote \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the
135	  matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). One has \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\).
136	\end{definition}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}
139	[Cocycle condition for the transition maps]
140	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleCondition}
142	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart}
143	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
144	  % affine patches" (read from
145	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
146	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
147	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
148	  %  we leave it to the reader to verify that
149	  %  for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
150	  %  cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
151	  %  $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
152	  %  is satisfied."
153	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
154	  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
155	  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
156	  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
157	  \[
158	    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
159	  \]
160	  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
161	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
162	  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
163	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
164	  glue the charts.
165	\end{lemma}
166	
167	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
168	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
169	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
170	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
171	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
172	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
173	% L838-L848.)
174	\begin{proof}
175	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart}
176	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
177	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
178	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
179	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
180	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
181	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
182	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
183	
184	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
185	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
186	  \[
187	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
188	  \]
189	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
190	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
191	  \[
192	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
193	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
194	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
195	  \]
196	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
197	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
198	  \[
199	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
200	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
201	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
202	  \]
203	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
204	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
205	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
206	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
207	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
208	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
209	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
210	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
211	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
212	\end{proof}
213	
214	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
215	\label{sec:gr_glued}
216	
217	\begin{definition}
218	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
219	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
221	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
222	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
223	  % affine patches" (read from
224	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
225	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
226	  % "Therefore the schemes
227	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
228	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
229	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
230	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
231	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
232	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
233	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
234	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
235	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
236	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
237	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
238	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
239	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
240	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
241	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
242	  \[
243	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
244	  \]
245	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
246	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
247	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
248	
249	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
250	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
251	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
252	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
253	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
254	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
255	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
256	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
257	  follows.
258	\end{definition}
259	
260	\section{Separatedness}
261	\label{sec:gr_separated}
262	
263	\begin{lemma}
264	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
265	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
267	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
268	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
269	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
270	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
271	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
272	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
273	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
274	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
275	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
276	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
277	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
278	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
279	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
280	\end{lemma}
281	
282	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
283	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
284	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
285	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
286	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
287	\begin{proof}
288	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
289	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
290	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
291	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
292	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
293	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
294	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
295	  homomorphism is surjective.
296	
297	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
298	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
299	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
300	  \[
301	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
302	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
303	    \qquad
304	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
305	  \]
306	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
307	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
308	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
309	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
310	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
311	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
312	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
313	  and in particular
314	  \[
315	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
316	  \]
317	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
318	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
319	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
320	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
321	  \[
322	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
323	  \]
324	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
325	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
326	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
327	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
328	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
329	\end{proof}
330

332	\label{sec:gr_proper}
333	
334	\begin{lemma}
335	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
336	  \label{lem:gr_proper}
337	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper}
338	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated}
339	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from

372	
373	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is
374	% proper. It is enough to verify the valuative criterion of properness for
375	% discrete valuation rings. Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let

381	% and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$ lies in $GL_d(\K)$. Now consider the homomorphism
382	% $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
383	% $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$ Then $g$ defines the same morphism

387	% $X^J$. Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ factors uniquely via
388	% $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of schemes
389	% $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
390	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked separatedness of
391	% $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
392	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891.)
393	\begin{proof}
394	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
395	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
396	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
397	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
398	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
399	  \[
400	    \begin{array}{ccc}
401	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
402	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
403	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
404	    \end{array}
405	  \]
406	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
407	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
408	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
409	
410	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
411	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
412	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
413	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
414	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
415	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
416	  \(K\). Define
417	  \[
418	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
419	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
420	  \]
421	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
422	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
423	  landing in \(U^J\).
424	
425	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
426	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
427	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
428	  \[
429	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
430	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
431	    \qquad
432	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
433	  \]
434	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
435	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
436	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
437	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
438	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
439	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
440	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
441	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
442	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
443	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
444	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
445	
446	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
447	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
448	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
449	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
450	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
451	  proper.
452	\end{proof}
453	
454	\section{Out of scope}
455	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
456	
457	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
458	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
459	and properness -- the GR-cells and GR-glue sub-phases. The following are
460	deliberately deferred to later sub-phases and are not treated here:
461	\begin{itemize}
462	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
463	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
464	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding (GR-quot).
465	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
466	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
467	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
468	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\) (GR-repr); this is blueprinted as
469	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
470	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
471	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
472	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
473	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
474	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
475	    that base change.
476	\end{itemize}
477

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60

63	% to a 3-field structure

65	%     `  sheaf        : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace`
66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`
67	%     `  coequifibered : NatTrans.Coequifibered`
68	%     `    (Functor.whiskerLeft (AffineZariskiSite.toOpensFunctor X).op unit.hom)`

70	% commit `b80f227`'s Stacks-01LL `NatTrans.Coequifibered` predicate is strictly
71	% weaker than the full `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` claimed by Definition
72	% 1.1's prose but is equivalent for the geometric purpose (constructing the
73	% relative-spectrum scheme) via the dense-subsite equivalence

75	% `AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData` consumes, so the iter-179
76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}

160	\begin{definition}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Structure morphism of the relative spectrum]
164	  \label{def:relspec_structure_morphism}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.structureMorphism}
166	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
167	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
168	  The \emph{structure morphism}
169	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) is the canonical projection of the
170	  relative spectrum to its base, obtained as the colimit descent of the affine-local
171	  structure morphisms \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \to U\) over the directed affine open
172	  cover of \(X\). It is the morphism whose affineness and base-change behaviour are the
173	  subject of the universal-property and base-change results below.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\begin{proof}
177	
178	\leanok
179	  Constructed directly in Lean as the base morphism of the relative gluing data of
180	  \(\mathcal{A}\); this is the projection underlying \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
181	\end{proof}

183	\begin{theorem}\leanok
184	[Universal property of the relative spectrum]
185	  \label{thm:relative_spec_univ}
186	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}
187	  % NOTE iter-283: removed the spurious `thm:relative_spec_base_change` from this
188	  % \uses. The universal property is the PRIMITIVE statement, proved by reducing
189	  % to the affine-base case and gluing (Lean body of `UniversalProperty` uses

194	  % `leanblueprint web`.
195	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, thm:relative_spec_affine_base,
196	    def:relspec_structure_morphism}

199	  % structural consequence ``IsAffineHom (structureMorphism 𝒜)'' rather than
200	  % the natural-bijection statement pinned by this prose. Iter-174+ must
